moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, claiming the vital center and preparing to develop our pieces harmoniously. This move controls the important d5 and f5 squares while freeing our light-squared bishop and queen for potential development. The e4 advance is the most direct way to fight for central space and creates immediate threats that our opponent must address. By occupying e4, we're inviting Black to respond symmetrically with e5, but we should be prepared for more provocative defenses that challenge our central pawn immediately.

Our opponent chooses the provocative Alekhine Defense, immediately attacking our central e4 pawn rather than establishing their own central presence. This hypermodern approach aims to provoke us into advancing our pawns aggressively, hoping to later demonstrate that our extended pawn center is overreached and vulnerable. Black's knight on f6 controls the important e4 and d5 squares while preparing to retreat to safety when we advance our pawn. While this defense can lead to sharp tactical play, it requires precise knowledge since Black often ends up with a cramped position if not handled carefully.

We're choosing the quiet Maróczy Variation instead of the more aggressive pawn advances like e5 or f4, opting for solid development over immediate space-gaining. This move supports our e4 pawn solidly while preparing to develop our light-squared bishop to e2 or d2, maintaining flexibility in our setup. The d3 advance also prepares f2-f4 as a future possibility and avoids the sharp theoretical battles that arise from more forcing continuations. While this approach is less aggressive than the main lines, it leads to positions where we maintain a solid center and can gradually improve our position without allowing Black the counterplay that typically arises from overextended pawns.
